slides.title: When the Network Talks, Who's Listening?
slides.subtitle: Detecting Attacks on Networks: the automated tools, AI, and detection methods that turn millions of log lines into an alert — and the trade-offs between catching every attack and drowning in false alarms
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Welcome to Unit 3, Securing Networks, and Lesson 3.5, Detecting Attacks on Networks. Earlier topics in this unit protected and configured the network; this one asks a different question — once an attacker is inside or knocking, how does an organization actually notice? This single comprehensive deck covers all five learning objectives, 3.5.A through 3.5.E. We start with the automated tools that listen — NIDS, NIPS, and SIEM — then move to how AI scales that listening across tens of millions of log lines, how teams choose a detection method, how they weigh its impact, and finally how an analyst reads log files to name a specific attack. The whole topic turns on one trade-off: catch every attack, or avoid drowning in false alarms.

slides.heading: By the End of This Topic You Will...

items.text: Identify the automated tools that detect network attacks — NIDS, NIPS, and SIEM — and tell apart 'just alert' from 'alert and act' (LO 3.5.A)
Explain how organizations leverage AI to classify millions of log data points and how alert thresholds balance missed attacks against false alarms (LO 3.5.B)
Determine a detection method — signature, anomaly, or hybrid — from traffic volume, pattern consistency, data sensitivity, and likelihood of novel attacks (LO 3.5.C)
Evaluate a detection method's impact by speed, cost, false-positive rate, and false-negative rate (LO 3.5.D)
Apply detection techniques to log files to identify indicators of specific network attacks (LO 3.5.E)

slides.script: Five objectives for this topic, one per learning objective. First, 3.5.A — identify the automated detection tools and tell apart a tool that only alerts from one that can also act. Second, 3.5.B — explain how AI scales detection and how an organization's alert threshold trades off missed attacks against false alarms. Third, 3.5.C — determine the right detection method from four criteria. Fourth, 3.5.D — evaluate that method's impact across speed, cost, false positives, and false negatives. And fifth, 3.5.E — read a log file and name the specific attack from its indicators. Keep these five in view; everything today hangs on them.

slides.prompt: Two security tools watch the same network. Tool A spots malicious traffic and immediately raises an alert for a human to investigate — but it does nothing else. Tool B spots the same traffic and, on its own, closes the port and blocks the attacker's IP address to stop the attack.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: what is the key difference between Tool A and Tool B? Which one would you want guarding a hospital's life-support network, and what is the risk of letting a tool act automatically without a human in the loop?
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own, no notes. Read it: Tool A detects malicious traffic and raises an alert, but takes no action. Tool B detects the same traffic and acts on its own — closes the port, blocks the IP. In three to five sentences, name the key difference, and decide which you'd put on a hospital network, and what the risk is of letting a tool act automatically. Hold your answers. By the end of today you'll know that Tool A is a NIDS and Tool B is a NIPS, and you'll be able to cite the exact EK that separates them.

slides.heading: What You'll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Guided Notes follow the five learning objectives of this topic. Green = CB required. Blue = enrichment.

sections.title: The Listening Tools (LO 3.5.A)
sections.description: Log files as the raw material, then NIDS, NIPS, and SIEM — detect, detect-and-halt, and aggregate-and-alert (EK 3.5.A.1–A.4).

sections.title: AI and Thresholds (LO 3.5.B)
sections.description: Why humans can't read millions of log lines, how AI classifies them, and how a likelihood threshold balances missed attacks vs. false alarms (EK 3.5.B.1–B.4).

sections.title: Choosing & Judging a Method (LO 3.5.C–D)
sections.description: Signature, anomaly, or hybrid — chosen by four criteria and judged by speed, cost, false positives, and false negatives (EK 3.5.C.1–C.4, D.1–D.4).

sections.title: Reading the Logs (LO 3.5.E)
sections.description: Match an indicator in a log file to the specific network attack it reveals, from evil-twin to smurf to network-based IoCs (EK 3.5.E.1–E.7).

sections.title: Enrichment: The Trade-off Triangle
sections.description: See how speed, cost, and coverage pull against each other so no single method 'wins' (EK 3.5.C, D).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 3.5 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Your notes follow the five objectives. Section one, the listening tools — log files plus NIDS, NIPS, and SIEM. Section two, AI and thresholds — why humans can't keep up and how a threshold tunes alerts. Section three folds together choosing a method and judging its impact. Section four is reading logs to name an attack. And the blue enrichment section ties it together with the trade-off triangle. Keep your pencil moving; I collect Guided Notes at the end.

slides.label: The Tools That Listen: NIDS, NIPS, SIEM
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Networks · Learning Objective 3.5.A
slides.script: Section one. Learning objective 3.5.A. Before any tool can detect an attack, it needs data — and that data lives in log files. Then we'll meet the three named tools: NIDS, NIPS, and SIEM. Watch for what each one does once it finds something.

slides.heading: Same Data, Two Jobs: Detect vs. Detect-and-Halt
slides.subheading: EK 3.5.A.1–A.3 — both tools read the same logged data, but only one can act to stop an attack.

left.title: NIDS — Network Intrusion Detection System (EK 3.5.A.2)
left.definition: Analyzes data, then alerts — but does not act
examples: Automated detection tools analyze data collected from the network and devices, often in a log file (EK 3.5.A.1)
A NIDS analyzes that data to determine if malicious activity is taking place
When an attack is detected, the NIDS generates an alert
Key limit: a NIDS does NOT take action to stop the attack

right.title: NIPS — Network Intrusion Prevention System (EK 3.5.A.3)
right.definition: Analyzes data, alerts, AND can stop the attack
examples: Like an IDS, a NIPS analyzes data to determine if malicious activity is taking place
Unlike a NIDS, a NIPS can also mitigate or halt the attack
It does so by closing ports, blocking specific IP or MAC addresses, or rejecting specific protocols
Detection vs. detection-plus-response is the whole difference

slides.footer: AP TIP: If a tool 'detects and alerts' but takes no action, it's a NIDS (EK 3.5.A.2). If it 'detects and can also mitigate or halt' the attack, it's a NIPS (EK 3.5.A.3). The verb 'act/halt/block' is your tell.
slides.script: Two columns, same logged data, two different jobs. On the left, EK 3.5.A.1 and A.2: automated tools analyze data collected from the network and devices, usually in a log file, and a NIDS — network intrusion detection system — analyzes that data to determine if malicious activity is happening, then generates an alert. Crucially, a NIDS only alerts; it does not stop the attack. On the right, EK 3.5.A.3, the NIPS — network intrusion prevention system — does everything the NIDS does, but can also mitigate or halt the attack by closing ports, blocking IP or MAC addresses, or rejecting protocols. The tell on the exam is the verb: if the tool acts, it's a NIPS. Write both EKs.

slides.heading: SIEM: One Pane of Glass Over Many Sources (EK 3.5.A.4)
bullets: A SIEM — security information and event management system — collects and analyzes data from MANY sources at once: firewalls, NIDS/NIPS, device logs, and application logs (EK 3.5.A.4)
It looks across those sources to detect PATTERNS that may indicate a cyberattack — connections no single tool would catch alone
When it finds such a pattern, the SIEM raises an alert (EK 3.5.A.4)
The alert is not the end: security analysts INVESTIGATE it to decide whether it is a true threat (EK 3.5.A.4)
Analysts then follow standard operating procedures to resolve the alert or escalate it (EK 3.5.A.4)
So the workflow is: aggregate sources → detect a pattern → alert → human investigates → resolve or escalate
slides.script: Where a NIDS or NIPS watches one stream, a SIEM — security information and event management system — pulls together data from many sources at once: firewalls, the NIDS and NIPS, device logs, and application logs. By correlating across all of them, it spots patterns that no single tool would catch alone, and raises an alert when a pattern may indicate a cyberattack. But the alert is just the start: EK 3.5.A.4 is explicit that a human security analyst investigates the alert to decide whether it's a true threat, then follows standard operating procedures to resolve it or escalate it. Memorize that workflow — the exam tests the analyst's role, not just the tool.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary — Detection Tools (LO 3.5.A)

terms.definition: Where automated tools collect the data they analyze — data from the network and devices such as switches, routers, servers, firewalls, and user computers (EK 3.5.A.1).
A network intrusion detection system; an automated tool that analyzes data to determine if malicious activity is taking place and generates an alert, but does not act to stop the attack (EK 3.5.A.2).

A network intrusion prevention system; like an IDS it analyzes data for malicious activity, but it can also mitigate or halt an attack by closing ports, blocking IP or MAC addresses, or rejecting protocols (EK 3.5.A.3).

A security information and event management system that collects and analyzes data from multiple sources to detect patterns that may indicate a cyberattack and raises an alert for analysts to investigate (EK 3.5.A.4).

slides.script: Four terms for the tools. A log file is where automated tools collect the data they analyze, from across the network and its devices. A NIDS analyzes that data and alerts, but takes no action. A NIPS does the same and can also halt the attack. A SIEM aggregates many sources, finds patterns, and raises an alert for a human analyst to investigate. Each definition carries its EK number — get all four into your notes by name and EK.

slides.label: Leveraging AI for Detection
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Networks · Learning Objective 3.5.B
slides.script: Section two. Learning objective 3.5.B. The tools in section one generate an overwhelming amount of data — far more than any human team can read. This section is how organizations leverage AI to keep up, and the single dial that controls whether they catch attacks or drown in false alarms.

slides.heading: Why Humans Can't Do This Alone (EK 3.5.B.1–B.2)
bullets: Computers log every action users take, and firewalls, IDS, IPS, and other sensors log all traffic passing through the network (EK 3.5.B.1)
A medium-sized organization's network logs MILLIONS — or even tens of millions — of data points per day (EK 3.5.B.1)
Even a large team of humans is incapable of analyzing that much data (EK 3.5.B.1)
So threat-detection teams create AI algorithms to analyze the large volume of data (EK 3.5.B.2)
The AI's job is to classify the data patterns as either malicious or normal (EK 3.5.B.2)
slides.script: Start with the scale problem. EK 3.5.B.1: computers log every user action, and firewalls, IDS, IPS, and other sensors log all the traffic through the network — for a medium-sized organization that's millions, even tens of millions, of data points a day. The CED is blunt: even a large team of humans cannot analyze that much data. So, EK 3.5.B.2, threat-detection teams build AI algorithms to analyze the data and classify each pattern as malicious or normal. AI isn't a luxury here; it's the only way to read the volume. Write both EKs.

slides.heading: Probabilities and the Threshold Dial
slides.subheading: EK 3.5.B.3–B.4 — the AI reports a likelihood, and the organization decides when that likelihood fires an alert.

left.title: Probabilistic Scoring (EK 3.5.B.3)
left.definition: The AI reports a likelihood, not a yes/no
examples: AI threat-detection models are based on probabilistic calculations
They report a PERCENTAGE indicating the likelihood that something is malicious
Example: 'this connection is 78% likely to be malicious'
It is a probability, not a certainty — which is why a threshold is needed

right.title: The Alert Threshold (EK 3.5.B.4)
right.definition: The org sets the percentage that triggers an alert
examples: Organizations determine their OWN thresholds for what likelihood results in an alert
Threshold set too HIGH → real attacks may go undetected
Threshold set too LOW → the team is overwhelmed with false alerts
Tuning the threshold is a balance, not a 'higher is safer' dial

slides.footer: AP TIP: The threshold is an ORGANIZATIONAL decision, not a fixed technical setting (EK 3.5.B.4). Too high misses real attacks; too low buries the team in false alarms. Be ready to reason about both failure modes.
slides.script: Two columns on how AI decides. On the left, EK 3.5.B.3: these models are probabilistic — they don't say malicious or not, they report a percentage likelihood that something is malicious, say 78 percent. On the right, EK 3.5.B.4: the organization sets its own threshold for what percentage triggers an alert. And here's the heart of it — set the threshold too high and real attacks slip past undetected; set it too low and the team is buried in false alerts. The threshold is a balance an organization chooses, not a fixed setting. Expect the exam to ask you to reason about both failure modes. Write both EKs.

slides.heading: A Higher AI Threshold Is Not Always Safer
slides.misconception: Setting the AI alert threshold very high makes the network safer because there are fewer false alarms.
slides.correction: EK 3.5.B.4 says a threshold set too high means real attacks may go undetected, while a threshold set too low overwhelms the team with false alerts. Tuning the threshold is a balance between missing attacks and drowning in alarms — not a 'higher is safer' dial.
slides.whyItHappens: Fewer alerts feels calmer and safer, so it's tempting to crank the threshold up. But fewer alerts can mean real attacks are sliding past unnoticed.
slides.example: At a very high threshold, a genuine attack scored at 60% likelihood never fires an alert — a missed attack hiding behind a quiet dashboard.
slides.script: A common misconception: students assume a higher AI threshold is always safer because there are fewer false alarms. EK 3.5.B.4 says otherwise. Too high, and real attacks go undetected; too low, and the team is overwhelmed with false alerts. It's a balance. A quiet dashboard can mean you're safe — or it can mean a real attack scored just below your threshold and never alerted. Don't read 'fewer alerts' as 'more secure.'

slides.label: Determining a Detection Method
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Networks · Learning Objective 3.5.C
slides.script: Section three. Learning objective 3.5.C. Now the choice: signature-based, anomaly-based, or hybrid detection? The CED gives four criteria for deciding. We'll lay them out in a table, then compare the two base methods head to head.

slides.heading: Four Criteria → Which Method (EK 3.5.C.1–C.4)
slides.context: The CED names four criteria for choosing a detection method. Match the situation to the method the CED prefers.
rows: Signature-based (more efficient, runs faster; compares to a database of known IoCs/signatures that must be updated)
Anomaly-based (compares to a baseline of recorded activity; alerts when data is outside a tolerance range)
Especially sensitive or critical data or services
Hybrid (combines signature + anomaly; more expensive; generates more alerts)
New, never-before-seen attacks likely; hybrid cost prohibitive
Anomaly-based (signature CANNOT detect a new attack)
slides.note: Baselines for anomaly-based detection must be recorded on UNCOMPROMISED systems to establish expected data types and volumes (EK 3.5.C.2).
slides.script: Here's the decision table — four criteria from EK 3.5.C. Criterion one, traffic volume: high volume favors signature-based detection, which is more efficient and faster because it compares data to a database of known indicators of compromise, called signatures, that must be kept updated. Criterion two, pattern consistency: consistent traffic favors anomaly-based detection, which compares against a baseline of recorded activity and alerts when data falls outside a tolerance range — and that baseline must be recorded on an uncompromised system. Criterion three, sensitivity: more sensitive or critical networks lean hybrid, combining both, at higher cost and more alerts. Criterion four, novel attacks: signature-based detection cannot catch a brand-new attack, so when novel attacks are likely and hybrid is too costly, anomaly-based is preferred. Copy this table — it's the spine of 3.5.C.

slides.heading: Signature vs. Anomaly, Head to Head
slides.subheading: EK 3.5.C.1–C.2 — two base methods with opposite strengths; hybrid combines them.

left.title: Signature-Based Detection (EK 3.5.C.1)
left.definition: Matches data to a database of known signatures
examples: Compares detection data to a database of known indicators of compromise (IoCs), called signatures
More efficient for networks with HIGH traffic volume
Signature databases must be updated with IoCs for the latest attacks
Runs more quickly than anomaly-based detection

right.title: Anomaly-Based Detection (EK 3.5.C.2)
right.definition: Compares data to a baseline of normal activity
examples: Most effective on networks with CONSISTENT traffic patterns
Compares detection data to a baseline of recorded activity
Baselines must be recorded on uncompromised systems (expected data types and volumes)
Triggers an alert/action when data is outside a specified tolerance range

slides.footer: AP TIP: Hybrid detection (EK 3.5.C.3) combines BOTH — chosen for sensitive/critical networks — but it is the most expensive option and generates the most alerts.
slides.script: Now the two base methods side by side. Left, signature-based, EK 3.5.C.1: it compares incoming data against a database of known indicators of compromise — signatures — so it's efficient and fast on high-volume networks, but the database has to be constantly updated with the latest attacks. Right, anomaly-based, EK 3.5.C.2: instead of a known-bad list, it compares against a baseline of normal recorded activity, and that baseline must come from an uncompromised system. It works best where traffic is consistent, and it alerts when data falls outside a tolerance range. Hybrid combines both for sensitive networks — most coverage, but most expensive and most alerts. Write both EKs and the hybrid footer.

slides.label: Evaluating a Method's Impact
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Networks · Learning Objective 3.5.D
slides.script: Section four. Learning objective 3.5.D. Choosing a method isn't free — every choice has consequences. The CED evaluates impact on four factors: speed, cost, false positives, and false negatives. This is where the trade-offs become real.

slides.heading: Four Factors That Judge a Detection Method
slides.subheading: EK 3.5.D.1–D.4 — speed, cost, false positives, and false negatives — and how the methods compare on each.

items.title: Speed
items.summary: Faster detection enables faster response. Signature-based detection is faster than anomaly-based detection, especially on networks with high traffic volume.

items.title: Cost
items.summary: Tools and ongoing costs must fit a budget. Anomaly-based systems need more expensive hardware than signature-based; hybrid is the most expensive because it combines both methods.

items.title: False positive rate
items.summary: Signature-based has almost no false positives; anomaly-based or hybrid have higher rates — wasting time on nonmalicious activity and causing alert fatigue, where responders assume alerts are false before investigating.

items.title: False negative rate
items.summary: A false negative is when an adversary bypasses detection. Signature-based systems are easier to bypass than anomaly-based or hybrid, and false negatives can let adversaries cause loss, harm, disruption, or destruction.

slides.script: Four impact factors, EK 3.5.D.1 through D.4. Speed: faster detection means faster response, and signature-based is faster, especially on high-volume networks. Cost: anomaly hardware costs more than signature, and hybrid is the most expensive of all because it runs both. False positive rate: signature-based has almost no false positives, while anomaly and hybrid raise more — wasting effort on harmless activity and breeding alert fatigue, where responders stop taking alerts seriously because they assume they're false. False negative rate: a false negative is when an adversary slips past detection entirely, signature-based systems are the easiest to bypass, and a false negative can let an adversary cause real loss, harm, disruption, or destruction. Notice the tension: signature wins speed, cost, and false positives — but loses on false negatives and novel attacks. Write all four.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary — Impact & Errors (LO 3.5.D)

terms.definition: An alert on activity that is not actually malicious; anomaly-based and hybrid detection have higher false-positive rates than signature-based (EK 3.5.D.3).
A condition where responders get accustomed to false positives and take alerts less seriously, assuming alerts are false before investigating them (EK 3.5.D.3).

When an adversary can bypass a detection system; signature-based systems are easier to bypass, and false negatives can result in adversaries causing loss, harm, disruption, or destruction (EK 3.5.D.4).
Combining signature-based and anomaly-based detection; the most expensive option, generating more alerts, chosen for more sensitive or critical networks (EK 3.5.C.3).

slides.script: Four terms for impact and errors. A false positive is an alert on activity that isn't actually malicious — common with anomaly and hybrid. Alert fatigue is what happens when too many false positives train responders to dismiss alerts before investigating. A false negative is the opposite failure — an adversary bypasses detection entirely, and signature-based systems are the easiest to bypass. And hybrid detection is the combine-both approach: best coverage, highest cost, most alerts. Get all four into your notes with their EKs — false positive and false negative are opposite failure modes, and the exam tests that distinction.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Choosing and Judging a Method
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 3.5.C and 3.5.D. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A high-volume ISP needs the fastest possible detection and keeps its indicator database constantly updated; budget is tight. Name the detection method that fits, cite the EK, and state one impact-factor downside it accepts (cite that EK too).
Explain the difference between a false positive and a false negative. For each, name which detection method tends to produce more of it and one real consequence, citing the relevant EK.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: that high-volume ISP, tight budget, fast detection, updated signature database — name the method, cite the EK, and name one downside it accepts with its EK. You should land on signature-based, EK 3.5.C.1 and D.1, accepting more false negatives, EK 3.5.D.4. Question two: explain false positive versus false negative — which method produces more of each, and one consequence apiece, with EKs. Be precise and cite. This is exactly how the exam asks it. Go.

slides.label: Reading the Logs: Indicators of Attacks
slides.kicker: Detecting Attacks on Networks · Learning Objective 3.5.E
slides.script: Section five. Learning objective 3.5.E. The payoff: an analyst reads a log file and names the attack. Each network attack leaves a fingerprint, and the CED gives you the indicator to look for. We'll table them, then look at network-based indicators of compromise.

slides.heading: Indicator → Attack: A Detection Cheat Sheet (EK 3.5.E.1–E.6)
slides.context: When analyzing log files and network traffic, match the indicator to the attack it reveals.
rows: Scan for SSIDs that look suspicious or similar to legitimate local SSIDs; signal triangulation to locate and disable the rogue access point
No wireless devices in a physical space can connect; scan for electromagnetic (EM) noise in the wireless range
Monitor traffic for unusual ARP messages, especially duplicate-MAC-address ARP packets; check the ARP table on the default gateway
Watch for an unexpected surge of Ethernet frames with different MAC addresses; check the MAC address table on a switch
Difficult to detect; an abrupt, inexplicable drop in website traffic means DNS records should be examined
Watch traffic for a sudden increase in ICMP requests sent to the network's broadcast address
slides.note: Sentence frame: 'The indicator ____ points to a(n) ____ attack, detected per EK 3.5.E.__ by ____.'
slides.script: Here's your indicator-to-attack cheat sheet from EK 3.5.E.1 through E.6. Evil-twin: scan for suspicious SSIDs that mimic legitimate ones, and use signal triangulation to find and disable the rogue access point. Jamming: nobody in a physical space can connect, and you scan for electromagnetic noise. ARP poisoning: unusual ARP messages, especially duplicate-MAC-address packets, and check the gateway's ARP table. MAC flooding: a surge of Ethernet frames with different MAC addresses, and check the switch's MAC table. DNS poisoning is hard to spot, but an abrupt, unexplained drop in website traffic says examine the DNS records. And smurf: a sudden spike in ICMP requests to the broadcast address. Memorize the indicator for each — that's exactly the 3.5.E exam task.

slides.heading: Network-Based Indicators of Compromise (EK 3.5.E.7)
bullets: Network-based IoCs are discovered when analyzing network traffic, often in the form of packet-capture files (EK 3.5.E.7)
Indicators can be found in source and destination IP addresses, ports, and protocols (EK 3.5.E.7)
Watch for connections to known malicious IP addresses (EK 3.5.E.7)
Watch for unusual spikes or slowdowns in network traffic (EK 3.5.E.7)
Watch for mismatched port-application traffic — a port that doesn't match its expected application (EK 3.5.E.7)
slides.script: Beyond the attack-specific tells, EK 3.5.E.7 gives you a general toolkit: network-based indicators of compromise, found by analyzing network traffic, often in packet-capture files. Look in the source and destination IP addresses, the ports, and the protocols. Four concrete red flags: connections to known malicious IP addresses, unauthorized network scans, unusual spikes or slowdowns in traffic, and mismatched port-application traffic — a port carrying traffic that doesn't match the application it should. When the exam hands you a packet capture, these four are what you're hunting for. Write all four indicators.

slides.heading: Enrichment: The Detection Trade-off Triangle
slides.subheading: Not a new EK — it shows why no single method wins on every factor at once.

left.title: Pull One Corner, Lose Another
left.definition: Speed, cost, and coverage fight each other
examples: Signature: fast and cheap with almost no false positives (EK 3.5.C.1, D.1, D.3)…
…but it can't catch novel attacks and is the easiest to bypass — more false negatives (EK 3.5.C.4, D.4)
Anomaly: catches novel attacks on consistent networks (EK 3.5.C.2, C.4)…
…but costs more hardware and raises more false positives → alert fatigue (EK 3.5.D.2, D.3)

right.title: Why Hybrid Isn't a Free Win
right.definition: Most coverage, highest cost, most alerts
examples: Hybrid combines both methods, so it covers the most ground (EK 3.5.C.3)
But it is the most expensive option of all (EK 3.5.D.2)
And it generates the most alerts, deepening false-positive load (EK 3.5.C.3, D.3)
So even 'do both' is a trade-off, reserved for the most sensitive/critical networks

slides.footer: TAKEAWAY: There is no free lunch. The 'right' method depends on the EK 3.5.C criteria, and every choice carries an EK 3.5.D impact you accept on purpose.
slides.script: Quick enrichment that ties the whole topic together — the trade-off triangle. Signature-based is fast, cheap, and barely ever cries wolf, but it can't see a novel attack and it's the easiest to bypass, so it accepts more false negatives. Anomaly-based flips that: it catches the brand-new attack on a consistent network, but it costs more and floods you with false positives, which breeds alert fatigue. And hybrid? It covers the most ground by doing both — but it's the most expensive and generates the most alerts, so even 'do both' is a trade-off you only make for the most sensitive networks. The lesson: there's no free lunch. You pick a method from the 3.5.C criteria and you accept a 3.5.D impact on purpose.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Read the Log, Name the Attack
slides.subheading: Both questions map to LO 3.5.E. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A log review shows duplicate-MAC-address ARP packets, and the analyst checks the default gateway's ARP table. Name the attack, cite the EK, and contrast its indicator with the indicator for a MAC flooding attack.

In a packet-capture file an analyst finds connections to a known malicious IP address and traffic where the port doesn't match its expected application. Name the category these findings fall under, cite the EK, and list one other indicator in that same category.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent. Question one: duplicate-MAC-address ARP packets plus checking the gateway's ARP table — name the attack, cite the EK, and contrast it with the MAC flooding indicator. That's ARP poisoning, EK 3.5.E.3, versus a surge of Ethernet frames with different MAC addresses on a switch, EK 3.5.E.4. Question two: connections to a known malicious IP and a port that doesn't match its application — name the category and cite the EK, then add one more indicator from it. That's network-based IoCs, EK 3.5.E.7, and you could add unauthorized scans or unusual traffic spikes. Cite as you go. Go.

slides.heading: Detecting Attacks on Networks, in One Slide
points: Tools (3.5.A): a NIDS detects and alerts; a NIPS detects and can halt; a SIEM aggregates many sources, alerts, and a human analyst investigates (EK 3.5.A.1–A.4)

AI (3.5.B): networks log millions of points a day, so AI classifies them as malicious or normal, reporting a likelihood; the organization's threshold balances missed attacks (too high) against false alarms (too low) (EK 3.5.B.1–B.4)
Choosing (3.5.C): four criteria — high volume → signature; consistent patterns → anomaly; sensitive/critical → hybrid; novel attacks → anomaly when hybrid is too costly (EK 3.5.C.1–C.4)

Impact (3.5.D): signature is faster, cheaper, and almost no false positives, but easier to bypass (false negatives); anomaly/hybrid catch more but cost more and cause false positives and alert fatigue (EK 3.5.D.1–D.4)
Reading logs (3.5.E): match the indicator to the attack — evil-twin, jamming, ARP poisoning, MAC flooding, DNS poisoning, smurf — and hunt packet-capture files for network-based IoCs (EK 3.5.E.1–E.7)
slides.next: Scenario 3B asks you to recommend detective controls for a wireless network and describe their impact — exactly the 3.5.C choose-a-method and 3.5.D judge-its-impact skills you built today.
slides.script: Everything in one slide. Tools: NIDS detects and alerts, NIPS can also halt, SIEM aggregates and a human investigates. AI: too much data for humans, so AI classifies it and reports a likelihood, and the organization's threshold balances missing attacks against false alarms. Choosing: four criteria steer you to signature, anomaly, or hybrid. Impact: signature is fast, cheap, and clean on false positives but easier to bypass; anomaly and hybrid catch more but cost more and cry wolf more. And reading logs: match the indicator to the attack, and hunt packet captures for network-based IoCs. That sets up Scenario 3B — recommend detective controls and describe their impact, which is exactly what you practiced. Review your Guided Notes; you should be able to do all five of these cold.

quiz.title: Lesson 3.5 — Detecting Attacks on Networks — Quiz
quiz.instructions: Choose the single best answer. 14 questions, 25 minutes, no notes.

questions.stem: A security team installs an automated tool on its network. The tool analyzes traffic to determine whether malicious activity is taking place, and when it detects an attack it generates an alert — but it takes no action to stop the attack. Which tool is described?
options: A network intrusion detection system (NIDS), which analyzes data for malicious activity and raises an alert when an attack is detected
A network intrusion prevention system (NIPS), which closes ports and blocks IP or MAC addresses to halt an attack
A security information and event management (SIEM) system, which collects data from many sources to detect patterns
A baseline recorder, which captures expected data types and volumes on an uncompromised system
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.A.2 says a network intrusion detection system (NIDS) is an automated tool that analyzes data to determine if malicious activity is taking place on a network, and when an attack is detected it generates an alert — but it does not act to stop the attack. Option B describes a NIPS (EK 3.5.A.3), which can also mitigate or halt an attack. Option C describes a SIEM (EK 3.5.A.4). Option D describes anomaly-detection baselining (EK 3.5.C.2), not a detection tool category.

questions.stem: After a NIDS alerts on suspicious traffic, the team wants a tool that not only detects the malicious activity but can also actively mitigate or halt the attack on its own. Which capability distinguishes a NIPS from a NIDS, per the CED?
options: A NIPS only stores log files for later review, while a NIDS analyzes them in real time
A NIPS collects data from firewalls, NIDS, and application logs to detect patterns across sources
A NIPS can mitigate or halt an attack by closing ports, blocking specific IP or MAC addresses, or rejecting specific protocols
A NIPS reports a probability percentage indicating how likely the activity is to be malicious
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.A.3 says a NIPS, like an IDS, analyzes data to determine if malicious activity is taking place, but a NIPS can also mitigate or halt an attack by closing ports, blocking specific IP or MAC addresses, or rejecting specific protocols. Option A inverts the tools and is false. Option B describes a SIEM (EK 3.5.A.4). Option D describes AI probabilistic scoring (EK 3.5.B.3), not a NIPS.

questions.stem: An analyst's dashboard shows an alert generated after a system pulled together data from the firewall, the NIDS/NIPS, device logs, and application logs and found a pattern that may indicate a cyberattack. According to EK 3.5.A.4, what should the analyst do with this alert?
options: Automatically close all ports and block every IP address that appears in the underlying logs
Investigate the alert to determine whether it is a true threat, then follow standard operating procedures to resolve or escalate it
Discard the alert, since a SIEM only aggregates data and cannot indicate a potential attack
Lower the AI detection threshold so that fewer alerts are generated in the future
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.A.4 says a SIEM collects and analyzes data from multiple sources to detect patterns that may indicate a cyberattack and raises an alert, and that security analysts investigate the alert to determine whether it represents a true threat and follow standard operating procedures to resolve or escalate it. Option A is the automated response of a NIPS, not the analyst's SIEM workflow. Option C contradicts the EK. Option D confuses SIEM workflow with AI thresholds (EK 3.5.B.4).

questions.stem: A medium-sized organization's network logs tens of millions of data points per day across firewalls, IDS, IPS, and other sensors. Leadership asks why a large human team alone cannot keep up. Per EK 3.5.B.1 and B.2, what is the CED's reasoning?
options: The data is encrypted, so humans are legally barred from reviewing the log files at all
Humans can review the data, but only if every device first deletes its logs each night
Even a large team of humans is incapable of analyzing so much data, so teams create AI algorithms to classify the data patterns as malicious or normal
Network sensors stop logging once volume exceeds a million data points, leaving little for humans to review
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.B.1 says firewalls, IDS, IPS, and other sensors log all traffic, that a medium-sized organization logs millions or tens of millions of data points per day, and that even a large team of humans is incapable of analyzing so much data. EK 3.5.B.2 adds that threat detection teams create AI algorithms to analyze the data and classify patterns as malicious or normal. Options A, B, and D invent rules the CED does not state.

questions.stem: An AI threat-detection model evaluates a connection and reports a 78% likelihood that it is malicious. The organization must decide whether this triggers an alert. Per EK 3.5.B.3 and B.4, what determines whether an alert fires?
options: The organization's own alert threshold — the percentage of likelihood it has decided should result in an alert
A fixed industry-standard threshold of exactly 50%, which every organization must use by law
Whether a human analyst has already manually reviewed the connection before the model scores it
The number of ports the NIPS has open, since closed ports raise the malicious-likelihood score
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.B.3 says AI threat-detection models are probabilistic and report a percentage likelihood that something is malicious, and EK 3.5.B.4 says organizations determine their own thresholds for what percentage of likelihood results in an alert. Whether 78% fires an alert depends on the organization's chosen threshold. Options B, C, and D invent constraints the CED does not state.

questions.stem: A team sets its AI detection threshold very high so it almost never alerts, hoping to reduce noise. Per EK 3.5.B.4, what is the risk of setting the threshold too high?
options: The security team will be overwhelmed with false alerts — every connection now triggers an alarm
The AI model will stop reporting probabilities and instead return only yes-or-no answers
Real attacks may go undetected — their likelihood scores fall below the high threshold
Signature databases will update too quickly for the network to keep pace
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.B.4 says if the threshold is set too high, real attacks may go undetected; if it is set too low, the security team will be overwhelmed with false alerts. A very high threshold lets genuine attacks slip past unalerted. Option A describes the too-low case, not too-high. Options B and D are not stated in the CED.

questions.stem: An ISP must inspect an enormous, high-volume stream of network traffic as quickly as possible and is willing to maintain a constantly updated database of known indicators of compromise. Per EK 3.5.C.1, which detection method best fits, and why?
options: Anomaly-based detection, which never needs its database of indicators updated
A hybrid approach, which is always the cheapest option for any network
Manual human review, which scales better than automated tools at high traffic volumes
Signature-based detection, which is more efficient for high-traffic networks and runs more quickly than anomaly-based detection
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.C.1 says volume of network traffic is a criterion for choosing a detection method: signature-based detection is more efficient for networks with high traffic volume, compares data to a database of known IoCs (signatures) that must be updated, and runs more quickly than anomaly-based detection. Option A misstates anomaly detection, which compares to a baseline (EK 3.5.C.2). Option B is false — hybrid is the most expensive (EK 3.5.C.3, D.2). Option C contradicts the value of automated tools.

questions.stem: A network has very consistent, predictable traffic patterns, and the organization suspects adversaries may attempt brand-new attacks it has never seen before. Per EK 3.5.C.2 and C.4, which detection method is preferred when the cost of hybrid detection is prohibitive?
options: Signature-based detection, since it is the only method that can detect a new attack
Anomaly-based detection, which compares data to a baseline and can flag novel attacks that have no existing signature
Disabling detection entirely until a signature for the new attack is published
Hybrid detection, which is always required whenever any novel attack is possible
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.C.2 says anomaly-based detection is most effective on networks with consistent traffic patterns and compares data to a baseline of recorded activity. EK 3.5.C.4 says signature-based detection cannot detect a new attack, so when novel attacks are suspected, anomaly-based detection is preferred when hybrid's cost is prohibitively high. Option A is backwards — signatures cannot catch new attacks. Option C is reckless and unstated. Option D ignores the prohibitive-cost condition.

questions.stem: A hospital network handles especially sensitive and critical patient data and services, and budget is not the primary constraint. Per EK 3.5.C.3, which detection approach will such a network likely consider, and what is a trade-off?
options: Signature-only detection, which generates more alerts than any other approach
Anomaly-only detection, which is always cheaper than signature-based detection
A hybrid approach combining signature- and anomaly-based detection — more expensive than either alone and generating more alerts
No detection at all, since sensitive networks are exempt from monitoring requirements
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.C.3 says networks with more sensitive or critical data or services will likely consider a hybrid approach that combines signature-based and anomaly-based detection, that hybrid is more expensive than either method alone, and that hybrid models generate more alerts. Option A misattributes high alert volume to signature-only. Option B is false — anomaly hardware is more expensive than signature (EK 3.5.D.2). Option D invents an exemption.

questions.stem: A manager compares detection methods on the factors the CED uses to evaluate impact. I. Signature-based detection is faster than anomaly-based detection, especially on high-volume networks. II. Signature-based detection has almost no false positives. III. Signature-based systems are harder to bypass than anomaly-based or hybrid systems. Which statements are consistent with EK 3.5.D.1, D.3, and D.4?
options: I and II only
I and III only
II and III only
I, II, and III
questions.rationale: Statement I matches EK 3.5.D.1 — signature-based detection is faster, especially on high-volume networks. Statement II matches EK 3.5.D.3 — signature-based detection has almost no false positives. Statement III is false: EK 3.5.D.4 says signature-based systems are EASIER to bypass than anomaly-based or hybrid systems, not harder. So only I and II are consistent.

questions.stem: A SOC running anomaly-based detection gets so many false positives that responders start assuming new alerts are false before investigating them, and stop taking alerts seriously. Per EK 3.5.D.3, what is this condition called and what causes it?
options: A false negative, caused by an adversary successfully bypassing the detection system
Alert fatigue, caused by a high false-positive rate that conditions responders to dismiss alerts as false before investigating
A signature mismatch, caused by an out-of-date indicator-of-compromise database
Threshold overflow, caused by setting the AI likelihood threshold far too high
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.D.3 says anomaly-based or hybrid detection has higher false-positive rates, and that alert fatigue occurs when responders get accustomed to false positives and take alerts less seriously because they assume alerts are false before investigating them. Option A defines a false negative (EK 3.5.D.4), a different concept. Options C and D are invented terms not in the CED.

questions.stem: An adversary manages to bypass an organization's detection system entirely, so the attack is never flagged and goes on to cause data loss. Per EK 3.5.D.4, what is this outcome called, and which system type is easiest to bypass this way?
options: A false positive; anomaly-based systems are the easiest to bypass
Alert fatigue; hybrid systems are the easiest to bypass
A baseline error; SIEM systems are the easiest to bypass
A false negative; signature-based detection systems are the easiest to bypass
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.D.4 says a false negative occurs when an adversary can bypass a detection system, that signature-based detection systems are easier to bypass than anomaly-based or hybrid systems, and that false negatives can result in adversaries causing loss, harm, disruption, or destruction. Option A misdefines a false positive. Option B misuses alert fatigue. Option C invents a term and the wrong system.

questions.stem: While analyzing network traffic, an analyst notices duplicate-MAC-address ARP packets and decides to check the ARP table on the default gateway. Per EK 3.5.E.3, which network attack do these indicators point to?
options: An ARP poisoning attack, detected by unusual ARP messages such as duplicate-MAC-address ARP packets and by checking the gateway's ARP table
A jamming attack, detected by scanning for electromagnetic noise in the wireless range
A smurf attack, detected by a sudden increase in ICMP requests to the broadcast address
An evil-twin attack, detected by scanning for suspicious SSIDs similar to legitimate ones
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.E.3 says ARP poisoning attacks can be detected by monitoring network traffic for unusual ARP messages — particularly duplicate-MAC-address ARP packets — and by checking the ARP table on the default gateway, exactly the indicators described. Option B is jamming (EK 3.5.E.2), Option C is a smurf attack (EK 3.5.E.6), and Option D is an evil-twin attack (EK 3.5.E.1) — each with different indicators.

questions.stem: Reviewing packet-capture files, an analyst sees connections to known malicious IP addresses, an unauthorized network scan, and traffic where the port does not match its expected application. Per EK 3.5.E.7, how are these findings best described?
options: Baseline recordings that must be captured only on compromised systems
Proof that signature-based detection has no false positives on this network
Evidence of a jamming attack, identified by electromagnetic noise in the wireless range
Network-based indicators of compromise (IoCs) found by analyzing network traffic, such as in packet-capture files
questions.rationale: EK 3.5.E.7 says network-based IoCs are discovered when analyzing network traffic, often in packet-capture files, and can be found in source/destination IPs, ports, and protocols — including connections to known malicious IP addresses, unauthorized network scans, unusual spikes or slowdowns, and mismatched port-application traffic. Option A misstates baselining (baselines are recorded on uncompromised systems, EK 3.5.C.2). Option B is unrelated. Option D describes jamming (EK 3.5.E.2).

guide.title: Lesson 3.5 — Detecting Attacks on Networks — Teacher Guide
guide.subtitle: Unit 3 · Topic 3.5 · 7 class periods · Scenario 3B
overview: Lesson 3.5 is the detection layer of Unit 3's network-security arc: after earlier topics protect and configure networks, this topic asks how an organization detects an attack on its network. Across seven days students identify the automated tools that detect network attacks — NIDS, NIPS, and SIEM (LO 3.5.A); explain how organizations leverage AI to enhance threat detection and response (LO 3.5.B); determine an appropriate network detection method from criteria such as traffic volume, pattern consistency, data sensitivity, and likelihood of novel attacks (LO 3.5.C); evaluate the impact of a detection method by speed, cost, false-positive rate, and false-negative rate (LO 3.5.D); and apply detection techniques to identify indicators of specific network attacks by analyzing log files (LO 3.5.E). The organizing idea is the trade-off triangle of detection: signature-based detection is fast, cheap, and low on false positives but cannot catch novel attacks and is easier to bypass; anomaly-based detection catches novel attacks but costs more and raises more false positives; hybrid does both at the highest cost.

Scenario 3B (Configuring a Secure Wireless Network) anchors the topic: students recommend a collection of detective controls to monitor the network and raise an alert for any potential malicious activity, and describe the impact of those detective-control recommendations — which maps directly onto LO 3.5.C (determine a method) and LO 3.5.D (evaluate its impact).
los: LO 3.5.A — Identify types of automated security tools used to detect network attacks.
LO 3.5.B — Explain how organizations can leverage artificial intelligence (AI) to enhance threat detection and response.
LO 3.5.C — Determine a network detection method.
LO 3.5.D — Evaluate the impact of a network detection method.
LO 3.5.E — Apply detection techniques to identify indicators of network attacks by analyzing log files.
eks: EK 3.5.A.1 — Automated detection tools analyze data collected from an organization's network and devices, such as switches and routers, servers, firewalls, and user computers. These data are often collected in a log file.

EK 3.5.A.2 — A network intrusion detection system (NIDS) is an automated tool that analyzes data to determine if malicious activity is taking place on a network. When an attack is detected, it generates an alert.

EK 3.5.A.3 — A network intrusion prevention system (NIPS) is an automated tool that, like an IDS, analyzes data to determine if malicious activity is taking place on a network. A NIPS can also mitigate or halt an attack by closing ports, blocking specific IP or MAC addresses, or rejecting specific protocols.

EK 3.5.A.4 — A security information and event management (SIEM) system collects and analyzes data from multiple sources (including firewalls, NIDS/NIPS, device logs, and application logs) to detect patterns that may indicate a cyberattack and raises an alert if a potential attack is detected. Security analysts investigate the alert to determine whether it represents a true threat and follow standard operating procedures to resolve or escalate the alert.

EK 3.5.B.1 — Computers log every action that users take. Firewalls, IDS, IPS, and other network sensors log all the traffic passing through various points in a network. A medium-sized organization's network is logging millions (or even tens of millions) of data points per day. Even a large team of humans is incapable of analyzing so much data.
EK 3.5.B.2 — Threat detection teams are creating AI algorithms to analyze large amounts of data and classify the data patterns as malicious or normal.
EK 3.5.B.3 — AI models for threat detection are based on probabilistic calculations; they report a percentage to indicate the likelihood that something is malicious.

EK 3.5.B.4 — Organizations determine their own thresholds for what percentage of likelihood of a threat results in an alert. If the threshold is set too high, real attacks may go undetected; if the threshold is too low, the security team will be overwhelmed with false alerts.

EK 3.5.C.1 — Volume of network traffic is a criterion for determining a detection method. Signature-based detection is more efficient for networks with high traffic volume. Signature-based detection compares detection data to a database of known indicators of compromise (IoCs), called signatures. Signature databases must be updated with IoCs for the latest attacks. Signature-based detection runs more quickly than anomaly-based detection.

EK 3.5.C.2 — Consistency of network traffic patterns is a criterion for determining a detection method. Anomaly-based detection is most effective on networks with consistent traffic patterns. Anomaly-based detection compares detection data to a baseline of recorded activity. Baselines must be recorded on uncompromised systems to establish expected data types and volumes. Anomaly-based detection triggers an alert or action when data types or volumes outside of a specified tolerance range are recorded.

EK 3.5.C.3 — Degree of sensitivity or criticality of a network is a criterion for determining a detection method. Networks with more sensitive or critical data or services will likely consider a hybrid approach. Hybrid detection combines signature-based and anomaly-based detection. Hybrid detection is more expensive than using either signature- or anomaly-based detection alone, and hybrid-detection models generate more alerts.

EK 3.5.C.4 — Likelihood of novel attacks on a network is a criterion for determining a detection method. Signature-based detection cannot detect a new attack. When an organization suspects that adversaries are likely to attempt a new attack on a network, anomaly-based detection is the preferred method when the cost of hybrid detection is prohibitively high.

EK 3.5.D.1 — Speed of detection is a factor in evaluating the impact of a network detection method. Faster detection enables faster response. Signature-based detection methods are faster than anomaly-based detection methods, especially on networks with high traffic volume.

EK 3.5.D.2 — Cost is a factor in evaluating the impact of a network detection method. Detection tools and ongoing costs need to be within a budget. Anomaly-based detection systems require more expensive hardware to operate than signature based. Hybrid detection is the most expensive option because it combines both anomaly- and signature-based methods.

EK 3.5.D.3 — False positive rate is a factor in evaluating the impact of a network detection method. Signature-based detection has almost no false positives. Anomaly-based or hybrid detection will have higher false positive rates. Impacts of high false positive rates include time and resources put toward investigating nonmalicious activity, and alert fatigue — a condition that occurs when responders get accustomed to false positives and take alerts less seriously because they assume alerts are false positives before investigating them.

EK 3.5.D.4 — False negative rate is a factor in evaluating the impact of a network detection method. A false negative occurs when an adversary can bypass a detection system. Signature-based detection systems are easier to bypass than anomaly-based or hybrid systems. False negatives can result in adversaries causing loss, harm, disruption, or destruction to data and systems.

EK 3.5.E.1 — Evil-twin attacks can be detected by regularly scanning for service set identifiers (SSIDs) that look suspicious or similar to local legitimate SSIDs. Signal triangulation can be used to locate and disable an access point broadcasting an evil-twin network.

EK 3.5.E.2 — Jamming attacks can be detected by recognizing that no wireless devices in a specific physical space are able to connect to a wireless network and by scanning for electromagnetic (EM) noise in the wireless range.

EK 3.5.E.3 — ARP poisoning attacks can be detected by monitoring network traffic for unusual ARP messages (particularly duplicate MAC address ARP packets) and checking the ARP table on the default gateway.
EK 3.5.E.4 — MAC flooding attacks can be detected by monitoring network traffic for an unexpected surge of Ethernet frames with different MAC addresses and checking the MAC address table on a switch.

EK 3.5.E.5 — DNS poisoning attacks are difficult to detect. However, if an organization's website experiences an abrupt and otherwise inexplicable drop in traffic, DNS records should be examined as a potential cause.
EK 3.5.E.6 — Smurf attacks can be detected by watching network traffic for a sudden increase in ICMP requests sent to the network's broadcast address.

EK 3.5.E.7 — Network-based IoCs are discovered when analyzing network traffic, often in the form of packet capture files. Indicators can be found in source and destination IP addresses, ports, and protocols, and can include connections to known malicious IP addresses, unauthorized network scans, unusual spikes or slowdowns in network traffic, and mismatched port-application traffic.

pacing.focus: Automated tools that detect network attacks: NIDS, NIPS, SIEM (LO 3.5.A)

segments.segment: Bell ringer: which tool just alerts, which can also act?
Guided-notes preview + objectives
Log files as the raw material of detection (EK 3.5.A.1)
NIDS vs. NIPS: detect vs. detect-and-halt (EK 3.5.A.2, A.3)
SIEM: aggregating sources and the analyst workflow (EK 3.5.A.4)
Vocab + Stop and Think
Wrap and assign Your Turn

pacing.focus: Leveraging AI for threat detection and response (LO 3.5.B)

segments.segment: Bell ringer: tens of millions of log lines a day
Why humans alone can't scale; AI classification (EK 3.5.B.1, B.2)
Probabilistic scoring and likelihood percentages (EK 3.5.B.3)
Thresholds: too high vs. too low (EK 3.5.B.4)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Determining a network detection method, part 1: signature vs. anomaly (LO 3.5.C)

segments.segment: Bell ringer: high-traffic ISP vs. predictable office network
Traffic volume → signature-based (EK 3.5.C.1)
Pattern consistency → anomaly-based and baselines (EK 3.5.C.2)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Determining a method, part 2: sensitivity, novel attacks, hybrid (LO 3.5.C)

segments.segment: Bell ringer: a hospital's most sensitive systems
Sensitivity/criticality → hybrid (EK 3.5.C.3)
Novel attacks → anomaly when hybrid is too costly (EK 3.5.C.4)
Criteria-matching practice + Stop and Think

pacing.focus: Evaluating the impact of a detection method (LO 3.5.D)

segments.segment: Bell ringer: fast and cheap, but what's the catch?
Speed and cost as impact factors (EK 3.5.D.1, D.2)
False positives and alert fatigue (EK 3.5.D.3)
False negatives and bypass risk (EK 3.5.D.4)
Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Applying detection techniques to log files (LO 3.5.E)

segments.segment: Bell ringer: read the indicator, name the attack
Wireless attacks: evil-twin and jamming (EK 3.5.E.1, E.2)
ARP poisoning, MAC flooding (EK 3.5.E.3, E.4)
DNS poisoning, smurf, network IoCs (EK 3.5.E.5, E.6, E.7)
Log-analysis Stop and Think + wrap

pacing.focus: Review, Scenario 3B detective-control plan, and quiz (LO 3.5.A–E)

segments.segment: Bell ringer: cumulative detection scenario
Review table: every tool, method, and indicator
Scenario 3B: recommend detective controls and describe their impact
Quiz debrief + bridge to next topic

pacing.label: Flex / Assessment block — no new deck (uses the Lesson Quiz)
pacing.focus: Not a numbered prep day. Run it as a block-period extension or as the next lesson’s warm-up (doubles as spaced retrieval). The quiz already ships as its own document, so no new deck or notes are needed.

segments.segment: Lesson 3.5 quiz

misconceptions.heading: NIDS and NIPS are the same thing
misconceptions.myth: A network intrusion detection system and a network intrusion prevention system do exactly the same job.
misconceptions.reality: Both analyze data for malicious activity, but EK 3.5.A.2 says a NIDS only generates an alert, while EK 3.5.A.3 says a NIPS can also mitigate or halt the attack by closing ports, blocking IP or MAC addresses, or rejecting protocols. Detection vs. detection-plus-response is the difference.

misconceptions.heading: A higher AI threshold is always safer
misconceptions.myth: Setting the AI alert threshold very high makes the network safer because there are fewer false alarms.
misconceptions.reality: EK 3.5.B.4 says a too-high threshold means real attacks may go undetected, while a too-low threshold overwhelms the team with false alerts. Tuning the threshold is a balance, not a 'higher is safer' dial.

misconceptions.heading: Signature-based detection is simply the best
misconceptions.myth: Since signature-based detection is fast, cheap, and has almost no false positives, it is always the right choice.
misconceptions.reality: EK 3.5.C.4 and D.4 note signature-based detection cannot detect a new attack and is easier to bypass (more false negatives). The right method depends on traffic volume, pattern consistency, sensitivity, and likelihood of novel attacks.

misconceptions.heading: False positives and false negatives are the same problem
misconceptions.myth: A false positive and a false negative are basically the same kind of error.
misconceptions.reality: EK 3.5.D.3 ties false positives to wasted effort and alert fatigue, while EK 3.5.D.4 says a false negative is when an adversary bypasses detection entirely and can cause real loss, harm, disruption, or destruction. They are opposite failure modes with different consequences.

support: Post a three-column word bank — 'signature: fast / cheap / few false positives / misses novel attacks', 'anomaly: catches novel / consistent-pattern networks / costlier / more false positives', 'hybrid: both / most expensive / most alerts' — so students match a scenario to a method instead of recalling all factors cold.
Provide a sentence frame for LO 3.5.E: 'The indicator ____ points to a(n) ____ attack, detected per EK 3.5.E.____ by ____.'
extension: Give students a short, invented packet-capture excerpt and have them flag every network-based IoC from EK 3.5.E.7 (malicious IPs, unauthorized scans, traffic spikes, mismatched port-application traffic) and justify each.

Have students design a detection plan for two contrasting networks — a high-volume ISP and a small but highly sensitive lab — and defend their signature/anomaly/hybrid choice using the EK 3.5.C criteria and the EK 3.5.D impact factors.

discussion: When is the inability of signature-based detection to catch novel attacks a dealbreaker, and when is it an acceptable trade for speed and cost?
Why does the CED frame AI thresholds as an organizational decision rather than a fixed technical setting?
Of the network attacks in LO 3.5.E, which are hardest to detect from log files, and why?
guide.answerKeyNote: Each question's full rationale is on the KEY copy of the quiz (print it with the answer-key option). The short answer key below is pulled straight from the quiz on this page, so it always matches the questions students see.
